card.tag_es: industria de medios · modelos de negocio
card.excerpt_en: For two decades, Meta and YouTube perfected the art of monetizing other people\
card.excerpt_es: Durante veinte años, Meta y YouTube perfeccionaron el arte de monetizar el trabajo ajeno. El modelo funcionó. El problema es que también tiene un techo.

header.label_es: Industria de Medios · Modelos de Negocio
header.deck_en: For two decades, Meta and YouTube perfected the art of monetizing other people\
header.deck_es: Durante veinte años, Meta y YouTube perfeccionaron el arte de monetizar el trabajo ajeno. El modelo funcionó. El problema es que también tiene un techo — y las dos empresas lo están tocando al mismo tiempo, desde lados opuestos.

sections.body_en:
Act I — The Problem

There is a number that neither Meta nor YouTube talk about much in their earnings calls, and it is the number that explains most of what they are actually doing right now.

YouTube Shorts generates 70 billion views per day. Instagram Reels has displaced the Feed as the dominant format on the platform. Both companies have won the short-form war (or at least the volume part of it). The problem is that winning at volume, in advertising, is not the same as winning at revenue. The average CPM for a YouTube Shorts placement runs around $3.50. A long-form YouTube video runs $10 to $20. A premium CTV placement runs $25 to $40. The format that is growing fastest inside both platforms is the format that monetizes worst.

This is what you could call the inventory paradox: more content, more distribution, less value per impression.

Chart 3 · The CPM problem
Short-form generates impression volume but significantly lower CPMs than long-form or CTV.
CTV premium YouTube long-form Instagram Reels YouTube Shorts
CTV $30, YouTube long-form $15, Reels $10, Shorts $3.50.
Sources: eMarketer 2025; DAC Group Media Inflation Report Q1 2025; Adwave Q4 2025
More content, distributed to more people, worth less per impression. The format growing fastest inside both platforms is the one that monetizes worst.

It is not a new insight for anyone who has bought programmatic inventory. What is new is the scale at which Meta and YouTube are now experiencing it, and the structural nature of the problem. The advertising model both platforms run on (impressions sold against user attention) has a ceiling that is invisible until you approach it. When inventory expands faster than advertiser budgets, CPMs compress. When you build a product whose value proposition is infinite free content, you have created the conditions for your own margin erosion. Both companies are arriving at that ceiling simultaneously, from different directions.

Chart 1 · Revenue growth
Meta vs. YouTube — annual revenue 2021–2025 (USD billions). Meta: advertising almost exclusively. YouTube: advertising + subscriptions.
Meta (ad revenue) YouTube (total) YouTube (ads only)
Meta: 2021 $115B, 2025 $201B. YouTube total: 2021 $29B, 2025 $60B.
Sources: Meta Platforms 10-K filings; Alphabet Q4 2025 earnings; Business of Apps 2026

Meta's advertising business generated $201 billion in 2025. That number is impressive enough that the ceiling is not obvious from the outside. But look at where the growth came from: AI-optimized ad delivery, recovering post-iOS14 targeting efficiency, and the strong H2 2025 rebound in advertiser budgets after two years of caution. The business is performing. The question is what happens when those tailwinds normalize, and you still have a product that is fundamentally a scale game — impressions at volume, CPMs held down by the very inventory you created.

YouTube has a slightly different version of the same problem. Its total revenue hit $60 billion in 2025, but the more interesting number is the split: roughly $40 billion from advertising, roughly $20 billion from subscriptions. That $20 billion from subscriptions is the fastest-growing part of the business — and it did not come from content in the traditional sense. It came from YouTube Premium (ad-free access), YouTube TV (live cable replacement), YouTube Music, and NFL Sunday Ticket. Google is paying the NFL as much as $2.5 billion per year for Sunday Ticket rights. They are doing it because it anchors YouTube as a must-have app on the living room TV (the highest-CPM advertising surface in the ecosystem). The content is the excuse. The TV screen is the point.

Chart 2 · Revenue mix
YouTube: subscription growth vs. advertising 2019–2025. Subscriptions grew from ~$3B (2019) to ~$20B (2025).
Ad revenue Subscriptions & other
Ad: 2019 $15B, 2025 $40B. Subscriptions: 2019 $3B, 2025 $20B.
Sources: Alphabet earnings calls 2019–2025; Variety Feb 2026; Business of Apps 2026

The diagnosis is this: both companies built their core businesses on a model that treats free content as an infinitely scalable input. They were right about the scale. They were late to understand that scale without pricing power is just volume — and volume alone does not compound.

Act II — The Strategies

The responses are logical, and structurally similar even though they look different on the surface.

Meta's move is threefold. First, it is paying creators — nearly $3 billion in 2025, up 35% from the prior year — to supply higher-quality content to Facebook, where the audience is large but the creator ecosystem has always been weak. The Creator Fast Track program offers guaranteed monthly payments to established creators from Instagram, TikTok, and YouTube who agree to post on Facebook. The goal is not to build a catalog. It is to rehabilitate a platform that has been losing cultural relevance for a decade by renting the social capital of people who built theirs elsewhere.

Second, Meta is building a subscription layer. Instagram Plus is in testing now — a premium tier for regular users that unlocks features like anonymous Story viewing and expanded audience controls. Separately, it is testing AI-powered premium features across Instagram, Facebook, and WhatsApp, building toward what looks like a consumer AI subscription, a product that competes less with Netflix and more with Microsoft 365. The bet is that AI capabilities are the first feature set that regular users will actually pay for inside a social platform.

Third, and most quietly, Meta's advertising business is being rebuilt by AI from the inside out. Advantage+ campaigns (AI-driven ad optimization) have become the primary vehicle for advertiser spend on the platform, and the performance improvement is real enough that it is pulling budgets from other channels. The near-term play is not subscriptions. It is making the existing inventory more valuable per impression through better targeting — buying time against the structural ceiling.

YouTube's strategy is easier to read because one number tells most of the story. Subscriptions went from $3 billion in 2019 to roughly $20 billion in 2025 — nearly a sevenfold increase in six years, achieved without launching a single original show that anyone would call essential television. The strategy is distribution, not content: if YouTube is where you watch everything else, and Premium removes the friction that makes that experience tolerable, the subscription sells itself. The NFL deal is an extension of the same logic. Sports is the last mass-audience content with genuinely inelastic demand. People pay for it even when they resent paying. Anchoring Sunday Ticket to YouTube TV locks a paying subscriber into the full platform stack. It is a $2.5-billion-a-year argument that the right content, at the right moment, converts casual users into paid ones.

The short-form problem is being managed rather than solved. YouTube is re-weighting its home feed toward Shorts (reducing long-form recommendations by up to 80% in some implementations), which increases Shorts view counts but also accelerates CPM compression on the inventory they can actually monetize well. It is a user retention play that creates an advertising problem. The tension between those two objectives is unresolved.

Act III — The Next Layer

Here is the thing neither company is quite saying, but both are moving toward: the advertising model is becoming a funding mechanism for a different business, not the business itself.

That is not a crisis. It is a transition — one that cable companies, mobile carriers, and console manufacturers have all navigated in different forms. The pattern is always the same: the original revenue model hits a structural ceiling, the company uses the cash it generates to build the next layer, and the next layer has better unit economics. Sony sold hardware, then used that to build a games library, then used that to build PlayStation Plus. The platform is the container; the subscription is the margin.

For YouTube, the logic is cleaner: the platform is already the winner in streaming by watch time, ahead of Netflix on TV screens for 17 consecutive months. The question is not whether people will pay — 125 million already pay for Premium, 100 million for Music. The question is what the next unlock is. And here is where YouTube keeps stopping short of the obvious move.

Both Meta and Alphabet built their businesses on a model that is, structurally, parasitic: they do not pay for the content that generates the attention they sell. Creators absorb production costs, platforms absorb advertising revenue, and the relationship is framed as a partnership because calling it what it actually is would be impolitic. Every show, every channel, every franchise that becomes valuable on YouTube belongs to the creator — not to Google. When MrBeast becomes the most-subscribed individual creator in platform history, YouTube gets the ad revenue and the subscription anchor. MrBeast gets the brand. If he leaves — or launches his own platform, which he has — YouTube loses the asset it never technically owned.

The companies that YouTube should be studying are not Netflix or HBO. They are the music labels.

A label does not produce music in the traditional sense — it finances, distributes, and licenses it, taking a share of rights in exchange. The model is capital-light relative to traditional studios, scales across a portfolio, and generates catalog value that compounds over time. YouTube has the data to do this better than any label ever could: it knows exactly which creators are building subscriber bases that convert to paid, which formats generate rewatch rather than single-view consumption, which topics are trending six months before they become mainstream. That is a development slate, if you choose to use it that way. The reason Alphabet has not is cultural as much as strategic: YouTube Originals launched in 2016, generated some genuine critical attention, and was quietly wound down by 2022. The reflex is to optimize the platform, not to own what runs on it.

For Meta, the transition is harder because the platform identity is social, not entertainment. People do not go to Instagram expecting to pay for something the way they might on YouTube or Netflix. The subscription layer Meta is building (AI features, anonymous viewing, expanded controls) has to answer a question that Snapchat and X have partially answered: there is a segment of power users who will pay for platform features if the features are genuinely useful and not available elsewhere. Snapchat hit 25 million Snapchat+ subscribers at $3.99 per month. Meta has a much larger user base and significantly more data on what each user actually does on the platform. The AI subscription, if built around genuine capability rather than feature gating, is the most plausible route to a second revenue stream that does not cannibalize the advertising core.

What each could learn from the other is also instructive. Meta has what YouTube lacks: the most efficient advertising machine ever built. Advantage+ does for targeting what YouTube has never systematically done — it converts behavioral data into CPM lift across any format, regardless of context. If YouTube could monetize Shorts with Meta's targeting sophistication, the CPM gap between short and long-form would close substantially. YouTube has what Meta lacks: genuine intent. When someone opens YouTube, they came to watch something specific. That intent is worth more per impression than social browsing, and it is the reason YouTube's long-form CPMs will always outperform Reels at equivalent scale. Meta cannot buy that psychology. It can only try to manufacture it, which is what all the creator spending is ultimately about.

The real innovation is not any individual product decision. It is the realization — which both companies are approaching from opposite sides — that the free-content model was a land grab, not an endgame. They grabbed an enormous amount of land. The question now is what you build on it.
sections.pullquote_en: More content, distributed to more people, worth less per impression. The format growing fastest inside both platforms is the one that monetizes worst.

coda_en: The lunch was never really free. Someone always paid. The interesting question is whether that's about to become visible on the balance sheet.
coda_es: El modelo nunca fue gratis. Alguien siempre absorbió el costo. La pregunta es cuándo eso deja de ser invisible — y para quién.
